questions.question: Explain why Ohm's law is not obeyed by a filament lamp. Describe the shape of its V-I graph and the reason behind it.
questions.answer: A filament lamp is non-ohmic. As current increases, temperature of the tungsten filament rises, increasing resistance (more collisions of electrons with vibrating atoms). Hence V-I graph is a curve bending upwards (resistance increases with V).

questions.question: A 6 Ω resistor is connected in series with a parallel combination of 4 Ω and 12 Ω resistors. Find the total resistance and current drawn from a 12 V battery.
questions.answer: Parallel part: 1/R_p = 1/4 + 1/12 = 4/12 ⇒ R_p = 3 Ω. Total R = 6 + 3 = 9 Ω. Current I = V/R = 12/9 = 1.33 A.

questions.question: Why do we prefer parallel connection over series for household wiring? Analyse what happens to power consumption if one appliance is switched off in each case.
questions.answer: Parallel: each gets full voltage, independent switching, failure of one doesn't affect others. In series, switching off one breaks circuit (all off). Power in parallel remains only for active appliances; in series, total power drops to zero if one is off.

questions.question: Derive an expression for the heat produced when current flows through a resistor using the concept of work done by electric field.
questions.answer: Work done W = V × Q, but Q = It, so W = VIt. Since V = IR, W = I²Rt. This work appears as heat (Joule's law of heating).

questions.question: Two bulbs rated 40 W-220 V and 100 W-220 V are connected in series across 220 V supply. Which bulb glows brighter and why?
questions.answer: In series, same current flows. Power P = I²R. Lower power bulb (40 W) has higher resistance (R = V²/P → 40 W has higher R). Higher R dissipates more power (I²R), so 40 W bulb glows brighter.

questions.question: A current of 5 A flows through a conductor of resistance 2 Ω for 2 minutes. Calculate (i) heat produced, (ii) charge flown.
Explain why the resistance of a semiconductor decreases with increase in temperature, while that of a metal increases.
questions.answer: In metals, increased temperature causes more lattice vibrations → more electron collisions → higher resistance. In semiconductors, higher temperature excites more electrons from valence to conduction band → more charge carriers → lower resistance.

questions.question: Three resistors of 2 Ω, 3 Ω and 6 Ω are connected in parallel. If a potential difference of 12 V is applied, find (i) total current, (ii) current in 3 Ω resistor.
Why is nichrome used in electric heaters while copper is used in electrical wiring? Compare their properties relevant to these uses.
questions.answer: Nichrome: high resistivity → produces more heat (H = I²Rt), high melting point, does not oxidise easily. Copper: very low resistivity → minimal heat loss during transmission, good conductor. Nichrome for heating; copper for conduction with low loss.

questions.question: A 220 V, 100 W bulb and a 220 V, 60 W bulb are connected in parallel to 220 V supply. Find the total power consumed and current drawn.
questions.answer: In parallel, each gets 220 V. Power total = 100 + 60 = 160 W. Current total = P/V = 160/220 ≈ 0.727 A.

questions.question: Explain the principle of an electric fuse and why it is connected in series with the live wire.
questions.answer: Fuse has low melting point alloy wire. High current (overload/short circuit) produces large heat → wire melts → circuit breaks. Connected in live wire so that when it melts, appliance is disconnected from high potential, preventing shock.

questions.question: Calculate the resistance of a heating element that takes 10 A current from 220 V supply and produces heat at the rate of 2000 W. Verify using Joule's law.
questions.answer: R = V/I = 220/10 = 22 Ω. Also, P = V²/R ⇒ R = V²/P = 48400/2000 = 24.2 Ω (mismatch due to approximation; exact match if values consistent). Alternatively, H/t = I²R → 2000 = 100 × R ⇒ R = 20 Ω (check consistency).

questions.question: Why does the coil of an electric stove become red hot while connecting wires remain cool? Analyse using Joule's law.
questions.answer: Stove coil (nichrome) has high resistance → large I²R heat. Connecting wires (copper) have low resistance → negligible heat for same current. Heat ∝ R for fixed I.

questions.question: In a circuit, a 4 Ω and 6 Ω resistor are in series with a 12 V battery. Find voltage drop across each and power dissipated in the 6 Ω resistor.
questions.answer: R_total = 10 Ω, I = 12/10 = 1.2 A. V across 4 Ω = 1.2 × 4 = 4.8 V; across 6 Ω = 1.2 × 6 = 7.2 V. Power in 6 Ω = I²R = 1.44 × 6 = 8.64 W.

questions.question: Explain why electrical power transmission is done at high voltage and low current.
questions.answer: Power loss in transmission lines = I²R (Joule heating). For same power P = VI, high V means low I → significantly reduced I²R loss. High voltage reduces energy wastage over long distances.

questions.question: A 2 kW heater is used for 3 hours daily. Calculate monthly bill at ₹7 per kWh (30 days). Also find resistance of heater if voltage is 220 V.
Why is an ammeter always connected in series and has very low resistance? What happens if it has high resistance?
questions.answer: Series connection to measure total current. Low resistance so it doesn't significantly increase circuit resistance or reduce current (doesn't alter reading). High resistance would decrease current and give wrong (lower) reading.

questions.question: Two 100 W bulbs are first connected in series and then in parallel to 220 V supply. Compare total power consumed in both cases.
questions.answer: Series: R each = V²/P = 484 Ω, total R = 968 Ω, I = 220/968 ≈ 0.227 A, P_total = I² × 968 ≈ 50 W (each ~25 W). Parallel: P_total = 100 + 100 = 200 W. Parallel consumes 4 times more power.

questions.question: Explain why the heating coil of an electric iron is made flat and broad while that of a toaster is coiled.
questions.answer: Iron: flat & broad to increase surface area for better heat transfer to clothes by conduction. Toaster: coiled to increase length in small space → higher resistance → more heat for glowing and toasting bread by radiation/convection.

questions.question: A circuit has three resistors: 5 Ω in series with parallel combination of 10 Ω and 15 Ω. If total current is 2 A, find voltage across the 5 Ω resistor.
questions.answer: Parallel: 1/R_p = 1/10 + 1/15 = 1/6 ⇒ R_p = 6 Ω. Total R = 5 + 6 = 11 Ω. V_total = I × R = 2 × 11 = 22 V. V across 5 Ω = I × 5 = 2 × 5 = 10 V.

questions.question: Why do we use MCBs (Miniature Circuit Breakers) instead of fuses in modern households? Give two advantages.
questions.answer: MCBs automatically trip on overload/short circuit and can be reset (reusable), unlike fuses which need replacement. They respond faster, are safer, and indicate fault position.

questions.question: Derive the relation between commercial unit (kWh) and joule. Calculate how many joules are there in 5 kWh.
In an experiment, V-I graph for a conductor is plotted. It is a straight line passing through origin but slope increases at higher currents. Explain the observation.
questions.answer: Straight line through origin shows Ohm's law at low currents (constant R). Increased slope at higher currents means decreased resistance? Wait—no: slope = 1/R. If slope increases, R decreases—possible in semiconductors or if temperature decreases (unusual). But typically for metals, slope decreases (R increases). Likely observation is for a thermistor or superconductor-like behaviour, but in Class 10 context, may indicate non-ohmic with temperature effect.

questions.question: A 1 kW geyser is used for 45 minutes daily. Calculate (i) energy consumed per day in kWh, (ii) monthly cost at ₹8 per unit (30 days).
Why is earthing provided in domestic electrical circuits? Explain what happens during a short circuit or leakage if earthing is present.
questions.answer: Earthing provides a low-resistance path to ground for leakage current. During short circuit or leakage, current flows to earth instead of through human body, tripping the fuse/MCB, preventing electric shock and fire.
